coverage_map.coverage_assessment: The numbers domain ignores pure number theory concepts like modular arithmetic, prime factor counting formulas, and complex divisibility rules. Instead, it acts as a test of exhaustive enumeration and recursive logic, requiring students to validate integer possibilities against compound constraints.

medium.steps_required: 3 to 5 steps (find all valid factors, test against a second constraint)
medium.concept_mixing: Integer properties combined with exponents or real-world constraints
medium.answer_similarity: Very high; wrong answers represent valid numbers that fail a secondary constraint

hard.concept_mixing: Sequences combined with complex exponent rules

distractor_engine.input_structure: Integer N, Primary rule (factors/consecutive), Secondary real-world constraint
distractor_engine.correct_transformation: Generate ALL factor partitions of N → Filter by Secondary Constraint → Extract targeted variable (max/min/left/right)
finite_distractor_set: Valid Primary / Failed Secondary — Premature termination on first valid partition, maps to M4Q17: 9
Wrong Variable Extraction — Returning the left page instead of the right page, maps to M1Q43: 78
Boundary Case Omission — Ignoring the (1,1,N) partition or the X^1 exponent case, maps to M1Q14: 16 exclusion failure

traps.name: Premature Termination
traps.mechanism: Student finds the first integer triplet satisfying the primary constraint and stops searching, missing the unique solution that satisfies the secondary constraint.

traps.name: Wrong Variable Extraction
traps.mechanism: Student correctly finds the pair of consecutive integers but returns the left-hand (smaller) page instead of the right-hand (larger) page.

traps.name: Boundary Case Omission
traps.mechanism: Student enumerates common factorizations but fails to include trivial cases (e.g., N^1, 1^N), missing a valid solution or incorrectly excluding a candidate.

M1Q5.A: Computed a3=9 correctly but used wrong formula for a4: 3×9-0=27 or arithmetic error → 9 and 18
M1Q5.B: Computed a3=9 correctly but used a4=3×9-0=27... or arithmetic error → 9 and 24
M1Q5.C: Off-by-one error in a3: 3×3-2=7? or wrong base values → 8 and 15
M1Q5.E: Student miscomputed a2 resulting in incorrect a3 value → 8 and 15 or 9 and 15

M1Q14.B: Appears in valid pair 4^5 — student confirmed it can appear
M1Q14.C: Student found some valid pairs and assumed all options are covered
M1Q14.D: Appears in valid pair 32^2 — student confirmed it can appear
M1Q14.E: Appears in valid pair 4^5 (5 is the exponent) — student confirmed it can appear

M1Q43.A: Incorrect square root estimation → tried 73×74=5402, not 6162
M1Q43.C: Wrong Variable Extraction — returned left page: 78×79=6162, selected 76 (arithmetic error)

M4Q17.A: Premature termination on (1,2,9) triplet → next year (2,3,10)=60≠76, but student selected 7 as max
M4Q17.B: Premature termination on (1,3,6) triplet → next year (2,4,7)=56≠76, selected 6 as max
M4Q17.C: Premature termination on some triplet → selected 8 as max
M4Q17.E: Premature termination on (1,2,9) → selected 9 as max without verifying next-year constraint

time_pressure_anatomy.description: Exhaustive search is highly time-consuming. Systematically listing all integer triplets that multiply to 18 (M4Q17) forces a methodical pace that is difficult to maintain under time pressure. M1Q43 requires estimating the square root of a 4-digit number (6162) without a calculator, inducing panic and guessing.

calibrated_uplift.key_principle: Bocconi difficulty = familiar problem + evil answer choices + slight ambiguity + time pressure.
calibrated_uplift.layer_1_problem_structure_mutation: Requiring basic factorizations to satisfy a secondary, delayed condition ('next year').
calibrated_uplift.layer_2_cognitive_load_injection: Forcing manual square root estimation of large integers.
calibrated_uplift.layer_3_answer_engineering: MOST WEIGHT. Structure options around valid intermediate numbers that represent the wrong variable or fail constraint 2. Every distractor must be a number that a student could plausibly reach via a specific enumeration error.

figure_passage_interaction_patterns.note: No figures. The text embeds risk through real-world anchoring. 'Open book' implies consecutive integers. 'Right-hand page' mathematically dictates the larger integer. 'Next year' mathematically dictates n1+1, n2+1, n3+1.

qualifier_inventory: cannot be either X or Y
ambiguity_patterns.primary_ambiguity_mechanism: 'CANNOT be either X or Y' is linguistically dense, forcing a dual-check mental verification. Real-world constraints act as implicit mathematical rules that must be inferred, increasing cognitive load before any arithmetic begins.
ambiguity_patterns.near_correct_construction: Second-closest options are valid solutions to the primary constraint that fail the secondary constraint, or correctly solved problems where the wrong variable is returned.

generation_fingerprint:
## NUMBERS GENERATION FINGERPRINT

The Numbers topic tests systematic enumeration and constraint satisfaction over abstract formulas. Dominant question types involve finding integer factors or consecutive numbers that satisfy two distinct, simultaneous rules. The distractor engine operates via a Dual-Constraint framework: the test generates distractors using valid numbers that perfectly satisfy the primary constraint but fail the secondary constraint, or represent the inverse variable in a paired solution (left vs right).

Primary trap mechanics involve Premature Termination (stopping the search after identifying the first valid mathematical case) and boundary case omission (ignoring trivial cases like N^1). Difficulty calibration mandates that the core algebra remains simple, but the search space is rigid and exhaustive.

The primary difficulty driver is not Numbers complexity but the precision required to distinguish answer choices engineered from specific reasoning errors.

Adhere to the Elimination Mapping Rule: every wrong option must correspond to a specific traceable student error — the generator must be able to state exactly which mistake leads to selecting each distractor.
